coredata.dc:title: Highly efficient and sensitive detection of tetracycline in environmental water: Insights into the synergistic mechanism of biomass-derived carbon dots and N-methyl pyrrolidone solvent
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Nie, Qi
Deng, Jingjing
Xie, Bing
Zhou, Tianshu
coredata.dc:description: The tetracycline (TC) residue in water environment has caused serious public safety issue. Thus, efficient sensing of TC is highly desirable for environmental protection. Herein, biomass-derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) synthesized from natural Ophiopogon japonicus f. nanus (O. japonicus) were used for TC detection. The unique solvent synergism efficiently enhanced detection sensitivity, and the detailed sensing mechanism was deeply investigated. The blue fluorescence of N-CDs was quenched by TC via static quenching and inner filter effect. Moreover, the enhancement of green fluorescence from deprotonated TC was firstly proposed and sufficiently verified. The solvent effect of N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP) and the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) with N-CDs achieved an instantaneous enhancement of the green emission by 64-fold. Accordingly, a ratiometric fluorescence method was constructed for rapid and sensitive sensing of TC with a low detection limit of 6.3 nM within 60 s. The synergistic effect of N-CDs and solvent assistance significantly improved the sensitivity by 7-fold compared to that in water. Remarkably, the biomass-derived N-CDs displayed low cost, good solubility, and desired stability. The deep insights into the synergism with solvent can provide prospects for the utilization of biomass-based materials and broaden the development of advanced sensors with promising applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass-derived carbon dots
Solvent synergism
Tetracycline detection
Enhanced sensitivity
FRET effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of tetracycline in environmental water samples
Preparation and characterization of biomass-derived N-CDs
Performance of the sensor toward tetracycline
Detection of tetracycline in environmental water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of N-CDs: (A) TEM image, (B) Size distribution, (C) XRD pattern, (D) FT-IR spectrum, (E) Fluorescence spectra, (F) UV–vis absorption, excitation, emission spectra and photos (inset).

(A) Fluorescence spectra and photos (inset) of N-CDs and N-CDs + TC, (B) Fluorescence spectra and photos (inset) of N-CDs + NMP and N-CDs + NMP + TC, (C) Comparison of fluorescence intensities and response, (D) Fluorescence spectra at 520 nm. Conditions: 1 % N-CDs, NMP (70 %, V/V), 50 μM TC, 10 mM HEPES (pH 9.0).

(A) HPLC results of 10 μM TC, (B) Fluorescence spectra of Iso-TC, (C) Fluorescence spectra of TC (200 μM) + Iso-TC, (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of TC, (E) Fluorescence spectra of TC, (F) Fluorescence spectra of TC mediated by pH, (G) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs + NMP + TC mediated by pH, (H) XPS spectra of C 1s, (I) Circular dichroism spectra. Conditions: NMP (70 %, V/V), 50 μM TC, 10 mM HEPES.

(A) Fluorescence spectra, (B) UV–vis absorption spectra, (C) Fluorescence spectrum of N-CDs and UV–vis absorption spectrum of NMP + TC, (D) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs + NMP and N-CDs + NMP + TC, (E) Fluorescence lifetime decay curves, (F) Responses in different solvents. Conditions: 50 μM TC, 10 mM HEPES (pH 9.0).
TAS analysis: (A) TC, (B) TC + NMP, (C) N-CDs + NMP + TC, (D) Representative kinetics traces at 410 nm. Conditions: 1 % N-CDs, NMP (70 %, V/V), 50 μM TC, 10 mM HEPES (pH 9.0).

(A) Fluorescence spectra, (B) Plot of response (F 520/F 425) versus TC concentration, (C) Stability of the detection performance in 50 μM TC, (D) Selectivity test, 10 μM for TCs, 20 mg/L for HA and 100 μM for other chemicals. Conditions: 1 % N-CDs, NMP (70 %, V/V), 10 mM HEPES (pH 9.0).
Scheme of ratiometric fluorescence detection for TC based on biomass-derived N-CDs and NMP.
Comparison of quantum dots-based methods for TC detection.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: IFE: inner filter effect; SQE: static quenching effect; FRET: fluorescence resonance energy transfer; DQE: dynamic quenching effect; PET: photo-induced electron transfer; ET: electron transfer; AE: antenna effect.

head.ce:title: Highly efficient and sensitive detection of tetracycline in environmental water: Insights into the synergistic mechanism of biomass-derived carbon dots and N-methyl pyrrolidone solvent

School of Ecological and Environmental Sciences, Shanghai Engineering Research Center of Biotransformation of Organic Solid Waste, Shanghai Key Lab for Urban Ecological Processes and Eco-Restoration, East China Normal University, 500 Dongchuan Road, Shanghai, 200241, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Ecological and Environmental Sciences
Shanghai Engineering Research Center of Biotransformation of Organic Solid Waste
Shanghai Key Lab for Urban Ecological Processes and Eco-Restoration

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The tetracycline (TC) residue in water environment has caused serious public safety issue. Thus, efficient sensing of TC is highly desirable for environmental protection. Herein, biomass-derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) synthesized from natural Ophiopogon japonicus f. nanus (O. japonicus) were used for TC detection. The unique solvent synergism efficiently enhanced detection sensitivity, and the detailed sensing mechanism was deeply investigated. The blue fluorescence of N-CDs was quenched by TC via static quenching and inner filter effect. Moreover, the enhancement of green fluorescence from deprotonated TC was firstly proposed and sufficiently verified. The solvent effect of N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP) and the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) with N-CDs achieved an instantaneous enhancement of the green emission by 64-fold. Accordingly, a ratiometric fluorescence method was constructed for rapid and sensitive sensing of TC with a low detection limit of 6.3 nM within 60 s. The synergistic effect of N-CDs and solvent assistance significantly improved the sensitivity by 7-fold compared to that in water. Remarkably, the biomass-derived N-CDs displayed low cost, good solubility, and desired stability. The deep insights into the synergism with solvent can provide prospects for the utilization of biomass-based materials and broaden the development of advanced sensors with promising applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: As a broad-spectrum antibiotic, tetracycline (TC) has been widely used in animal husbandry, aquaculture, and the treatment of human infectious diseases [1,2]. However, high consumption and abuse of TC has led to its excretion and release into the environmental water samples [3]. The antibiotic residue poses devastating risks to ecological systems, promotes bacterial resistance, and seriously threatens human health [4,5]. Therefore, the efficient and sensitive detection of TC is urgently required for environmental protection and public safety.

Over the past few decades, various analytical strategies have been established for TC detection, among which, the fluorescence (FL) method is highly efficient due to its ease of use and satisfactory sensitivity [6,7]. Based on the probe-target interactions, a variety of nanomaterials have been used to construct TC sensors, such as copper nanoclusters [8], metal-organic frameworks [9], and nucleotide/lanthanide coordination polymers [10]. Different from these single-emission probes [11], ratiometric fluorescent sensors can effectively eliminate interferences and provide self-calibration of the assay [12–14]. For example, a perovskite-europium hybrid fluorescence sensor (MAPbBr3@PbBr(OH)@SiO2-Cit-Eu) was fabricated for ratiometric sensing of TC with a detection limit of 11.15 nM [13]. A dual-emission probe was developed for highly selective visual quantification of TC by using the red fluorescence of gold nanoclusters as the response signal and the green fluorescence of copper nanoclusters as the reference emission [14]. However, some of the probes suffer from complex preparation process, high cost or potential toxicity, which would restrict their large-scale production and practical application in environmental fields.

As an emerging class of carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted extensive attention in analytical fields due to their tunable photoluminescence properties, good dispersibility, and high biocompatibility [15,16]. Alternative to chemical reagents, many natural products, could be used as precursors for the preparation of biomass-derived CDs, including feathers, plants, and fruits [17–19]. The natural products not only display the desired advantages of low cost, sustainability, environmental friendliness, and easy scalability, but also provide abundant heteroatom sources and functional groups to improve the optoelectronic properties, stability, water solubility and analytical performance of CDs [20,21]. In this context, a variety of natural materials have been used to prepare biomass-derived CDs for TC determination, such as white berries [22], pomelo peels [23] and passion fruit peels [24]. However, these methods still face low sensitivity and long detection time, which need to be improved in a further step. Previous studies have shown that the assistance of organic solvents is a simple strategy to improve the response of the assay [25,26]. For instance, Liu et al. employed antimonene quantum dots to realize sensitive and ratiometric fluorescence detection of TC based on the derivatization reaction of TC at different pH and the enhancement effect of the solvent medium [26]. They proposed that the fluorescence color change from blue to yellow-green was due to the decomposition of TC under alkaline conditions, and the ketone structure in N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP) solvent is indispensable for promoting the green emission. Nevertheless, the origin of the green fluorescence has not been sufficiently verified, and the detailed mechanism of the solvent affecting the response remains unclear yet. Therefore, further investigation is urgently needed to elucidate the sensing principle and to better exploit the advantages of fluorescent biomass-derived CDs for rapid and efficient detection.

Ophiopogon japonicus f. nanus (O. japonicus) is widely distributed and easily accessible in China. Here, O. japonicus was used as both the carbon and nitrogen sources to prepare fluorescent N-CDs. On this basis, a novel ratiometric fluorescence strategy was constructed for TC detection (Scheme 1 ). The detection mechanism of the solvent-enhancing sensitivity was disclosed and experimentally confirmed. The blue fluorescence of N-CDs was effectively quenched by TC, while the green emission was significantly enhanced via the synergistic effect induced by biomass-derived N-CDs and NMP solvent. According to the instantaneous inverse fluorescence variations, the TC residue in water samples could be analyzed in a convenient process. The sensing performance in different solvents were systematically carried out and the principle of the solvents affecting the response process was comprehensively investigated. The proposed method based on the simple solvent-assisted synergistic effect has high sensitivity, rapid response, and good selectivity, which could provide new thoughts for the design of more efficient assays in complex matrix.
Chemicals and apparatus are described in the Supporting Information.

Biomass-derived N-CDs were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method using O. japonicus as the only raw material. Briefly, 0.5 g of O. japonicus was ultrasonically cleaned in water, dried naturally, and cut into 5 cm pieces in length. 12 mL of water was added, and the mixture was kept at 160 °C for 8 h. After the reaction completed and cooled down to room temperature, the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and dialyzed (MWCO: 500 Da) for 24 h. Finally, the product inside the dialysis bag was collected and the purified N-CDs solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C before use.

700 μL of NMP and 10 μL of N-CDs were mixed with different concentrations of TC. The mixture was diluted to 1 mL with HEPES buffer (10 mM, pH 9.0) and allowed to react for 1 min at room temperature. Fluorescence emission spectrum was measured in the range of 360–700 nm under an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Similarly, NMP was replaced by water for control experiments. In the selectivity test, various competing substances were determined, including inorganic ions (Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+), biomolecules (Glu, GSH, Cys), natural organic macromolecules (HA), other tetracycline antibiotics (TCs, including OTC, CTC, and DOX) and other types of antibiotics (KAN, AZI, CFX, CIP, ERY, CAP).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of tetracycline in environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The environmental water samples were collected from the rivers near an aquaculture farm, an industry district, and Minhang local in Shanghai, respectively. Prior to testing, the samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove large particles and adjusted to pH 9.0. The pretreated water samples were measured and analyzed according to Section 2.2. The analytical results were also validated by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) system and the experimental parameters can be found in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of biomass-derived N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, the amount of raw material, reaction temperature and reaction time were optimized in the synthesis of N-CDs. As shown in Fig. S1, the highest fluorescence intensity was obtained with 0.5 g of O. japonicus and the reaction condition of 160 °C for 8 h. Seen from the TEM image, the as-prepared N-CDs were uniformly dispersed (Fig. 1 A) with an average particle size of 2.61 ± 0.41 nm (Fig. 1B). They have distinct lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (inset of Fig. 1A), corresponding to the (100) plane of the graphitic structure. The XRD pattern (Fig. 1C) of N-CDs displayed a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 22.12° assigned to the C (002) plane and the diffraction peak at 2θ = 42.82° was ascribed to the C (100) plane [27]. In the FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 1D), the characteristic peak at 3462 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of O–H/N–H and the peak at 1647 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of the CO bond. The peaks at 1432 cm−1 and 1070 cm−1 represented the stretching vibration of C–N and C–O, respectively. The XPS result presented three main peaks located at 285.08 eV, 400.08 eV, and 533.08 eV, which were attributed to the C, N, and O elements with contents of 77.84 %, 1.76 % and 20.40 %, respectively (Fig. S2A) [28]. The spectrum of C 1s can be divided into three peaks at 284.79 eV, 286.22 eV and 288.20 eV (Fig. S2B), corresponding to C–C/CC, C–N/C–O and CO, respectively. The characteristic peaks of N 1s can be divided into pyridine nitrogen at 399.70 eV, amino nitrogen at 400.20 eV and pyrrolic nitrogen at 401.80 eV (Fig. S2C). The fitted peaks of O 1s were located at 531.85 eV and 532.80 eV, confirming the presence of CO and C–OH/C–O–C groups (Fig. S2D). All the above results confirmed that the biomass-derived N-CDs contained abundant functional groups on the surface, including carboxyl, amino and hydroxyl groups, which would endow them with good solubility and stability in water samples. As a representative natural material, the inherent rich content of O and N in O. japonicus endowed N-CDs with plentiful functional groups. Moreover, using O. japonicus as the precursor, the biomass-derived N-CDs realized self N-doping without any subsequent chemical modification, which provided a convenient strategy to introduce high density of defects, optimize the chemical structure of N-CDs and produce more active sites for interacting with TC [18,29,30].

The optical properties of N-CDs were displayed in Fig. 1E. The emission peak of N-CDs showed an excitation-dependent behavior with increasing excitation wavelength from 310 nm to 430 nm, which may be related to the different size distributions and surface states in N-CDs [31]. As shown in Fig. 1F, the broad absorption peak at 270 nm was attributed to π-π* transition of aromatic CC, while the weak absorption around 320 nm was caused by n-π* transition of CO [32]. The maximal fluorescence excitation and emission wavelengths of N-CDs were 350 nm and 435 nm, respectively. The aqueous N-CDs solution appeared brownish yellow under daylight (left photo) and emitted blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (right photo).

Since stability was an important factor to evaluate the photoluminescence performance of N-CDs [33], several parameters were investigated (Fig. S3). In can be seen that the as-prepared N-CDs have good stability in the temperature range of 5–55 °C, pH range of 3–10, and NaCl concentration up to 200 mM. In addition, the N-CDs could retain 95 % of the fluorescence intensity after continuous irradiation with a Xenon lamp for 60 min. These results demonstrated that the biomass-derived N-CDs have desirable chemical and optical stability, which is advantageous for their practical application in environmental and biosensing fields.

To verify the feasibility of the assay, the response of N-CDs to TC was investigated separately in water and NMP-mixed solvents. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the presence of TC could quench the blue fluorescence of N-CDs and gave rise to green emission, accompanied by a fluorescence color change from blue to light green (inset photo). In the NMP-mixed medium (Fig. 2B), the addition of TC resulted in a strong increase of the emission peak at 520 nm, together with an obvious signal variation and fluorescence color turned to bright green (inset photo). By contrast, the introduction of NMP solvent increased the sensitivity of the assay by ∼7-fold compared to that in water (Fig. 2C). As TC exhibited weak green emission (pink curve), the synergistic effect induced by the coexistence of NMP and N-CDs resulted in a 64-fold enhancement of the emission peak at 520 nm (red curve) (Fig. 2D).

The fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-CDs was investigated in detail. As shown in Fig. S4A, the UV–vis spectrum of TC overlapped well with the excitation spectrum of N-CDs, indicating the existence of an inner filter effect in the system. Besides, an absorption peak at 354 nm appeared in the mixture of N-CDs and TC (Fig. S4B), indicating the formation of a new complex [34,35]. In the FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs + TC (Fig. S4C, blue curve), a broader and redshifted peak of O–H/N–H vibration at 3170 cm−1 was observed compared with the peak at 3462 cm−1 in N-CDs (black curve) and 3361 cm−1/3300 cm−1 in TC (red curve), demonstrating the contribution of hydrogen bond interaction between them [36]. The peaks at 1675 cm−1, 1616 cm−1 and 1036 cm−1 were ascribed to CO, CC and C–N vibrations in TC, respectively. They were shifted and showed a decreased broad peak at 1517 cm−1 after mixing with N-CDs, confirming the involvement of π-π stacking interaction. In addition, the fluorescence lifetime (Fig. S4D and Table S1) of N-CDs did not change after adding different concentration of TC. The above results confirmed that the quenching of blue fluorescence was attributed to both static quenching and inner filter effect [37].

Regarding the size change of the probe during sensing process, the morphology of N-CDs was determined by TEM measurement. Seen from Fig. S5, N-CDs maintained a particle size of 2–4 nm and were uniformly dispersed in the mixture of N-CDs-NMP and TC, indicating that the enhancement of green emission was not related to the aggregation behavior. According to previous report [38], TC (Fig. S6A) undergoes electron transfer and aromatic ring cleavage to form a degradation product named Iso-TC (Fig. S6B) under alkaline conditions. To investigate the effect of pH, TC at different pH was measured by HPLC. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the amount of TC at pH = 9.0 was 97.6 % after 3 h at room temperature. Liu et al. proposed that due to the lactone structure in Iso-TC, the lone pair of electrons in the oxygen atom would affect the conjugated system, thereby enhancing the green fluorescence [26]. If this is the case, the green emission should be increased with the addition of Iso-TC. However, as seen in Fig. 3B, Iso-TC displayed an emission peak at 420 nm. The fluorescence intensity increased with the increasing concentration of Iso-TC (0.0–5.0 mol%) in TC (pH = 9.0), while the green emission at 520 nm remained unchanged (Fig. 3C). Based on the above results, it can be concluded that the green fluorescence was not derived from Iso-TC species.

Consequently, the photochemical properties of TC at different pH conditions were explored in a further step. In the UV–vis spectra (Fig. 3D), the absorption peak of the unsaturated ketone structure in TC was shifted from 360 nm to 372 nm at pH 9.0, together with the characteristic peak of β-diketone appeared at 270 nm. As seen in Fig. 3E, the fluorescence of TC is weak at pH ≤ 7.0 with the maximum emission wavelength of 560 nm, whereas the intensity is greatly enhanced with an emission peak of 520 nm at pH > 7.0. The large Stokes shift at low pH was attributed to the excited state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT) process, which was usually observed in the presence of a proton acceptor ortho to the phenolic groups, such as the C11 keto group in TC. The spectroscopic changes were related to the deprotonation of the hydroxyl group on C12 to form deprotonated TC at pH 9.0 (TC‾, Fig. S6C). In this case, the absorption spectrum was red-shifted due to a modest stabilization of the TC‾ ground state in the resonance-delocalized anion. The blue shift of the emission spectrum came from the inhibition of the ESIPT process from the phenolic hydroxyl group to the oxygen at C11, with the excited state TC‾ decaying directly to the ground state via the radiation path [39].

TC is known to be an amphoteric compound containing dimethylamino, phenolic hydroxyl, and enol hydroxyl structures with pKa values of 3.32, 7.78, and 9.58, respectively (Fig. S6A) [40]. It can be converted between different ionic forms as shown below:
Eq. (1)
TCH 3 +
⇌
pK
a 1
− O 3 H
TCH 2 ±
⇌
pK
a 2
− O 12 H
TCH −
⇌
pK
a 3
− C 4
NH +
TC
2 −

The above equilibria indicated that the deprotonation/protonation of TC was reversible, thus the emission spectrum of TC can be modulated by adjusting the pH value of the medium (Fig. 3F). Notably, the fluorescence of N-CDs + NMP + TC showed a consistent switchable behavior with pH regulation (Fig. 3G), demonstrating that the green fluorescence was originated from deprotonated TC. In the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (Fig. 3H), the content of CO/O–CO increased from 10.06 % to 18.66 % in NMP + TC, attributing to the ketone structure of NMP. The characteristic peak of C–OH shifted from 286.51 eV to 286.31 eV (Δ = −0.20 eV), together with CO/O–CO shifted from 288.57 eV to 288.05 eV (Δ = −0.52 eV). The variation demonstrated that the presence of NMP increased the delocalization of electrons in the system, resulting in an enhanced shielding effect and a lower binding energy by increasing the density of the outer electron cloud [41]. Circular dichroism spectra showed similar signals at 320 nm for different mixtures (Fig. 3I), confirming that the conformation of the BCD rings in TC was not affected by the solvent medium. In contrast, negative signals appeared around 260 nm corresponding to the A ring in TC (black and red curves), indicating that TC adopted a distorted conformation in water. In the presence of NMP, this signal became positive (blue and pink curves), implying that TC exhibited an extended conformation in the aprotic solvent [42]. However, the conformational change would not directly affect the fluorescence of TC but can cause a red shift in the absorption spectrum [43]. The green fluorescence was enhanced with increasing NMP content (Fig. 4 A), along with a gradual redshift from 372 nm to 390 nm and a new peak at 408 nm in the absorption spectra (Fig. 4B). The shift led to a better overlap between the absorption spectrum of NMP + TC and the emission spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 4C), thereby facilitating the ratiometric fluorescence response (Fig. 4D). Furthermore, NMP had no effect on the fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs, whereas the presence of TC significantly shortened the lifetime (Fig. 4E and Table S2). Through the above analysis and comparison, it implied that the sensing mechanism is related to the favorable FRET effect in the presence of NMP [44,45].

The responses in different solvents were analyzed (Fig. 4F), and the result demonstrated that the sensitivity of the assay in aprotic solvents was generally better than that in protic solvents. The related parameters were summarized in Table S3, including dielectric constant (ε), hydrogen bond donating ability (α), hydrogen bond accepting ability (β) and viscosity. The order of sensitivity in various solvents indicated that the polarity and hydrogen bond donating ability played an important role in controlling the interaction and emission of TC in the N-CDs-TC complexes. The less hydrogen bonding resulted in an enhanced emission via motional restriction of TC, and the dipole-dipole interaction leading to resonance energy transfer was attenuated with increasing polarity of the solvents. The relationship was also suitable for the behavior in protic solvents, except for IPA, where the high viscosity of IPA may hinder the target-probe interactions. Considering its low toxicity, low cost, high solubility, and good stability, NMP was chosen in this study to improve the sensitivity of the assay in a simple but efficient manner.

To investigate the excited state dynamic process in the presence of NMP and N-CDs, femtosecond time-resolved TAS was performed. As shown in Fig. 5 A–C, the TAS results of TC, TC + NMP and N-CDs + NMP + TC displayed similar signal distributions. The negative signal <400 nm corresponded to ground state bleaching (GSB), the positive signal from 400 to 500 nm corresponded to excited state absorption (ESA), and the negative signal >500 nm was attributed to stimulated emission (SE) signal. The excited state kinetic decay curves were extracted from the global fitting analysis and the results were shown in Fig. 5D and Table S4. The short component τ 1 (<10 ps) was assigned to the fast solvent equilibration process of TC, and the medium component τ 2 was ascribed to the S 1 state of TC, which can return to the ground state by emitting fluorescence [46]. In the presence of NMP, τ 1 remained unchanged but τ 2 increased significantly. This can be explained because in protic solvents (e.g., water), the hydrogen bond interaction between TC and the solvent was more favorable for charge transfer and/or intramolecular rearrangement, resulting in an increase in the non-radiative transitions. In aprotic solvents, however, the non-radiative transitions were inhibited, thereby the fluorescence was largely enhanced, which were consistent with Fig. 4A [47]. The addition of N-CDs resulted in the appearance of a long-lived component τ 3, relating to the new exciton radiation process after the occurrence of FRET. As it is confirmed that FRET can serve as a competing pathway to provide a new channel for excited state decay except radiation transitions [48], the donor (N-CDs) in the excited state could transfer the energy to the receptor (TC) and enhance its green fluorescence significantly.

As is well known, the FRET process is governed by intermolecular dipole-dipole interactions and is highly dependent on the distance between the donor and the acceptor. Based on the Förster theory [49], the FRET efficiency can be expressed by the following equations:
Eq. (2)
E = 1 −
τ DA
/
τ D
Eq. (3)
E = 1 /
[
1 +
(
r /
R 0
)
6
]
Eq. (4)
R 0
= 0.211
[
κ 2
n
− 4
Φ J
( λ )
]
1 / 6
Eq. (5)
J
( λ )
=
∫ 0 ∞
F D
( λ )
ɛ A
( λ )
λ 4
d λ
where E is the energy transfer efficiency, R 0 is the Förster distance (in Å), κ 2 is the orientation factor (generally to be 2/3), n is the refractive index, Φ is the quantum yield of the donor, J(λ) is the spectral overlap integral of the donor emission spectrum and the receptor absorption spectrum,
F D
and
ɛ A
are the normalized fluorescence emission spectrum of the donor and the extinction coefficients of the receptor, respectively. Based on the above equations, the distance between the donor and the acceptor was calculated to be 39.0 Å, which was well within the requirement for distance in FRET (10–100 Å).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the sensor toward tetracycline
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve better analytical performance, the relevant experimental parameters were optimized prior to testing. As shown in Fig. S7, the highest response (F 520/F 425) was obtained at the N-CDs concentration of 1 %, pH value of 9.0, NMP volume of 70 % and reaction time of 1 min. It can be explained that under alkaline conditions, it is more favorable for the deprotonation of TC and the enhancement of the solvent effect induced by NMP. However, the sensitivity was decreased due to the rapid decomposition of TC at pH > 10.

Under the optimal conditions, the performance of the sensor was examined. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the blue fluorescence at 425 nm gradually decreased with the increasing concentration of TC, while the green fluorescence at 520 nm was significantly enhanced. Fig. 6B displayed a good linear correlation (R 2 = 0.998) between the fluorescence ratio (F 520/F 425) and the concentration of TC in the range of 0.01–20 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) is determined to be 6.3 nM, which is much lower than the maximum residue level (MRL) of 225 nM and 676 nM set by the European Union and U.S. Food and Drug Administration, respectively [7]. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 6C, the signal drifting was below 2 % in one month, indicating desired stability and repeatability of the assay. Compared with previous quantum dots-based TC sensors, including some CDs-based fluorescence detection methods (Table 1 ), the biomass-derived N-CDs probe demonstrated self-doping of nitrogen, efficient utilization of natural product resources, and are environmentally friendly, low cost and highly stable. Impressively, the N-CDs/NMP system exhibited a ratiometric signal response, a lower detection limit, and a shorter response time than most of the assays. Although some methods have comparable sensitivity using the probe such as CDs@Eu/MIPs and BCNO QDs, the proposed method displayed advantages of facile preparation, convenient sensing process and fast response. Unlike the assays that only use inner filter effect [23,50] or interaction with the target analyte (TC) [51] to quench the fluorescence of the probes, the assistance of NMP solvent in this work plays an important role in enhancing the sensitivity and efficiency of detection. Furthermore, the fluorescence of the N-CDs/NMP system displayed good reversible performance with more than three consecutive cycles of pH mediation (Fig. S8). This behavior was attributed to the specific pH-dependent property of the chemical, and further confirmed the green-emission enhancement was derived from the deprotonation of TC species under alkaline conditions.

To evaluate the performance of the sensor in environmental samples, the responses of common ions, small molecules, natural organic macromolecules, and other antibiotics were measured. As shown in Fig. 6D, these substances caused negligible interference in the N-CDs/NMP system, only the addition of TCs induced a significant response. The satisfactory recognition ability was attributed to several aspects: 1) The basic chemical structure of TCs (Fig. S9), which contains two systems of keto-phenolic chromophores linked to ring A and BCD cycles, is significant for their specific intrinsic fluorescence. 2) The abnormal Stokes shift associated with the deprotonation transformation of TCs under alkaline conditions and the inhibited non-radiative transitions in aprotic solvents such as NMP are prerequisites for the profound emission. 3) The multiple amino, hydroxyl groups and aromatic rings in TCs provide abundant binding sites, various interactions, and matched spectroscopy with the fluorescence probe (N-CDs), promoting the occurrence of highly efficient FRET process with the consequential ratiometric fluorescence response. Based on above analysis, it can be concluded that the chemical structure and photophysical properties of TCs are indispensable for the hydrogen/π-π stacking interactions and pH-dependent fluorescence spectroscopy in the N-CDs/NMP system, ensuring improved anti-interference performance in complex metrics. As for doxycycline, oxytetracycline and chlortetracycline, they can also be detected by the proposed sensing method because they belong to the same TC family with similar chemical structures (Fig. S9) and physicochemical properties (Table S5). The results demonstrated that the sensing method had good selectivity and could be further applied to the rapid screening of the TCs in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of tetracycline in environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the practicability of the method, the sensor was used to detect TC in environmental water samples. The samples were spiked with different concentrations of TC (100 nM (lower than the MRL), 0.50, 1.00, and 10.00 μM) and were analyzed by the ratiometric fluorescence and HPLC, respectively. As summarized in Table S6, the recoveries of the sensor were in the range of 92.0–103.8 % with RSD less than 7.6 % for different water samples. Note that the results of the developed fluorescence sensor were in good agreement with the HPLC method. These outcomes validated that the proposed detection method has good reliability and high accuracy, which showed great potential in environmental analysis applications.

In summary, biomass-derived N-CDs were synthesized using O. japonicus and achieved rich functional groups to increase contact possibilities for highly sensitive and rapid detection of TC. The constructed sensing strategy and detection mechanism are of great significance in revealing solvent synergism. The blue fluorescence of N-CDs was quenched by static quenching and inner filter effect, while the green emission originating from deprotonated TC was enhanced by inhibited non-radiative transitions in the aprotic solvent NMP. With the assistance of the solvent, the response was greatly enhanced by changing the conformation and excited state dynamics of TC, presenting favorable FRET effect with N-CDs and obvious ratiometric fluorescence performance. The synergistic effect improved the sensitivity by 7-fold and realized selective detection of TC with a low LOD of 6.3 nM and a short time of 60 s. The results suggest that biomass-derived materials afford a promising route for high-value utilization of natural products and rapid sensing. More importantly, the relationship between sensing performance and solvent properties (i.e., polarity, hydrogen donating ability, viscosity) can help to select suitable detection conditions and enhance sensitivity with high efficiency. The in-depth understanding of solvent synergism would provide insights into the expansion and applications for the development of novel strategies and convenient sensors.

Qi Nie: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Jingjing Deng: Methodology, Data curation. Bing Xie: Resources. Tianshu Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National Key R&D Program of China (2018YFC1901000, 2021YFA1201701).
